title: By Jonah Goldberg July 11, 2016 11:14 AM PT Share via Close extra sharing options Email Facebook X LinkedIn Threads Redd
raw_response: {"rating": 1.8, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial. While it offers critiques of both sides and acknowledges police abuse and bias in non-lethal encounters, the author ultimately emphasizes law-and-order priorities and delivers stronger, sustained criticism of liberals’ approach to policing and government. The selection and framing of evidence and arguments yield a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames “national conversations” as attempts to bully the public into a single narrative, reflecting skepticism typical of right-leaning critiques of progressive discourse; (2) He centers a law-and-order premise that the state’s first obligation is to protect safety and property and warns that vilifying police undermines the legitimacy of law—positions aligned with conservative priorities; (3) He portrays liberals as “obtuse,” with “boundless faith” in government except for police, and highlights the “irony” of supporting regulations while distrusting their enforcers—one-sided left-criticizing framing; (4) He elevates police professionalism by contrasting officers’ training favorably against “typical” bureaucrats or legislators, a pro-police, right-coded emphasis; (5) Through sourcing choices, he highlights research suggesting black suspects are not more likely to be shot than white suspects, which challenges a central left narrative (while acknowledging non-lethal bias), tilting the weight of evidence against the left’s position; (6) He argues that liberals who blame opponents’ rhetoric for violence should consider their own side’s influence, casting the left as hypocritical; (7) Although he rebukes a conservative figure’s argument about intra-community crime and validates concerns about state abuses toward African Americans, these balancing moves are fewer and less forceful than his critiques of the left, resulting in an overall Lean Right orientation."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial. While it offers critiques of both sides and acknowledges police abuse and bias in non-lethal encounters, the author ultimately emphasizes law-and-order priorities and delivers stronger, sustained criticism of liberals’ approach to policing and government. The selection and framing of evidence and arguments yield a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames “national conversations” as attempts to bully the public into a single narrative, reflecting skepticism typical of right-leaning critiques of progressive discourse; (2) He centers a law-and-order premise that the state’s first obligation is to protect safety and property and warns that vilifying police undermines the legitimacy of law—positions aligned with conservative priorities; (3) He portrays liberals as “obtuse,” with “boundless faith” in government except for police, and highlights the “irony” of supporting regulations while distrusting their enforcers—one-sided left-criticizing framing; (4) He elevates police professionalism by contrasting officers’ training favorably against “typical” bureaucrats or legislators, a pro-police, right-coded emphasis; (5) Through sourcing choices, he highlights research suggesting black suspects are not more likely to be shot than white suspects, which challenges a central left narrative (while acknowledging non-lethal bias), tilting the weight of evidence against the left’s position; (6) He argues that liberals who blame opponents’ rhetoric for violence should consider their own side’s influence, casting the left as hypocritical; (7) Although he rebukes a conservative figure’s argument about intra-community crime and validates concerns about state abuses toward African Americans, these balancing moves are fewer and less forceful than his critiques of the left, resulting in an overall Lean Right orientation.

explanation: This is an opinion/editorial. While it offers critiques of both sides and acknowledges police abuse and bias in non-lethal encounters, the author ultimately emphasizes law-and-order priorities and delivers stronger, sustained criticism of liberals’ approach to policing and government. The selection and framing of evidence and arguments yield a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames “national conversations” as attempts to bully the public into a single narrative, reflecting skepticism typical of right-leaning critiques of progressive discourse; (2) He centers a law-and-order premise that the state’s first obligation is to protect safety and property and warns that vilifying police undermines the legitimacy of law—positions aligned with conservative priorities; (3) He portrays liberals as “obtuse,” with “boundless faith” in government except for police, and highlights the “irony” of supporting regulations while distrusting their enforcers—one-sided left-criticizing framing; (4) He elevates police professionalism by contrasting officers’ training favorably against “typical” bureaucrats or legislators, a pro-police, right-coded emphasis; (5) Through sourcing choices, he highlights research suggesting black suspects are not more likely to be shot than white suspects, which challenges a central left narrative (while acknowledging non-lethal bias), tilting the weight of evidence against the left’s position; (6) He argues that liberals who blame opponents’ rhetoric for violence should consider their own side’s influence, casting the left as hypocritical; (7) Although he rebukes a conservative figure’s argument about intra-community crime and validates concerns about state abuses toward African Americans, these balancing moves are fewer and less forceful than his critiques of the left, resulting in an overall Lean Right orientation.
